preamble: The close front unrounded vowel [i] lowers to the near-open front unrounded vowel [æ], a common phonetic process occurring in stressed syllables under lengthening conditions or as part of broader vowel shift chains. This process is attested historically in Germanic languages and in contemporary sociophonetic variation.

examples.note: Lowering of long high front vowels in open syllable lengthening contexts, with further lowering to [æ] in some dialects and sociolinguistic contexts.
Sociophonetic lowering variation in certain American English dialects and rapid speech styles, particularly affecting stressed vowels in morphologically complex words.
Dialectal lowering of close front vowels in certain phonological environments, contributing to regional phonetic variation.
Historical lowering of close vowels in certain phonological contexts during medieval Scandinavian language evolution.
